retrieved_docs.title: Juvenile Xanthogranuloma  -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: As opposed to other xanthomatous diseases, juvenile xanthogranulomas are not associated with metabolic or lipid disorders. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Gallbladder Carcinoma -- Etiology -- Gallbladder Carcinoma Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Primary sclerosing cholangitis and inflammatory bowel disease [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Cholecystitis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The proliferation of bacteria in the gallbladder can lead to acute cholecystitis or pus collections. In some cases, the gallstone may erode into the duodenum and impact in the terminal ileum, presenting as gallstone ileus. Rarely the patient may develop emphysematous cholecystitis due to the presence of gas-forming organisms like clostridia, E.coli, and klebsiella. This presentation is most common in diabetics and carries a high mortality rate. The relationship between chronic cholecystitis and gall bladder cancer is controversial. Though chronic inflammation has been shown to be associated with increased risk of cancer [17] , the data on this is limited. Xanthogranulomatous cholecystitis is a variant of chronic cholecystitis in which continued inflammation leads to extensive thickening and fibrosis extending locally beyond the gall bladder wall. In this severe variant, the occurrence of complications like abscesses and fistulas are more common. It is considered a pre-malignant condition. Porcelain gallbladder tends to be asymptomatic in most cases. The association with malignancy is again controversial but the consensus is that it carries a slightly increased risk of cancer. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Xanthogranulomatous Pyelonephritis  -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A small study showed that a prolonged course of preoperative antibiotics (>28 days) helped reduce inflammation and facilitate laparoscopic surgery in patients with xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis. [56] Having a general surgeon available is recommended as unexpected involvement of the gallbladder, liver, biliary ducts, pancreas, bowel, duodenum, spleen, or intestines may be discovered.

retrieved_docs.title: Gallbladder Polyp -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Adenomyomatosis Biliary sludge Gallstones Gallbladder carcinoma Gallbladder metastases Tumefactive sludge

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatobiliary Tract Cancer(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Primary sclerosing cholangitis Secondary sclerosing cholangitis Recurrent pyogenic cholangitis AIDS-associated cholangiopathy Autoimmune pancreatitis-cholangitis syndrome Hepatobiliary inflammatory pseudotumor Mirizzi Syndrome Xanthogranulomatous cholecystitis or cholangitis Biliary sarcoidosis Chemotherapy-induced biliary sclerosis Intrabiliary metastasis

retrieved_docs.title: Juvenile Xanthogranuloma  -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Ophthalmology

retrieved_docs.title: Gallbladder Polyp -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most common type of gallbladder polyps are pseudo or cholesterol polyps. These account for 60% to 90% of all gallbladder polyps. They are not true neoplastic growths, but rather they are cholesterol deposits that form as projections on the inner lumen of the gallbladder wall. They are formed from precipitation of cholesterol or bile salts. Presence of cholesterol polyps may be indicative of pathologic gallbladder disease such as chronic cholecystitis. Inflammatory polyps account for 5% to 10% of all gallbladder polyps. They are associated with inflammation of the gallbladder mucosa and wall. Usually, this type is associated with repeated bouts of cholecystitis and acute biliary colic. Both pseudopolyps and inflammatory polyps carry close to a zero risk of developing a gallbladder cancer. These polyps rarely exceed 1 cm in diameter and are often multiple. True adenomatous gallbladder polyps are considered neoplastic. They are rare and are often associated with gallstones. They can range in size from 5 mm to 20 mm. Once the polyp reaches a size of greater than 1 cm, consideration needs to be made for cholecystectomy, because of the potential malignant increases above 1 cm. Adenomyomatosis is a more common true poly. It has classically been considered a benign lesion of the fundus of the gallbladder. However, recent findings suggest these lesions do have premalignant potential. Malignant polyps tend to be singular and more than 2 cm in diameter. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Juvenile Xanthogranuloma  -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline the risk factors for xanthogranulomas. Describe the typical presentation of a patient with a xanthogranuloma. Review the pathophysiology of xanthogranulomas. Explain interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication regarding the management of patients with xanthogranulomas. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyomatosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The cause of adenomyomatosis is unknown, although some have proposed that it is a response to chronic inflammation of the gallbladder. Since patients are most frequently diagnosed in their 50s, the idea of chronic inflammation as the etiology seems plausible. [3] [4] Adenomyomatosis of the gallbladder was initially recognized as a precancerous lesion; recent studies currently consider it a benign gallbladder alteration often associated with cholecystitis and cholecystolithiasis. Gallbladder carcinoma is an extremely malignant disease with a 5-year survival rate of less than 5 percent. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Gallbladder Carcinoma -- Etiology -- Gallbladder Carcinoma Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital biliary cysts and abnormal pancreaticobiliary anatomy [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Gallbladder Polyp -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Two percent to 12% of routine gallbladder pathology specimens may contain gallbladder polyps, but true adenomatous polyps are present in less than 0.5% of all gallbladder specimens. Cholesterol polyps show an increased cholesterol content and are associated with gallbladder cholesterolosis and sludge. Inflammatory polyps exhibit inflammation of the gallbladder wall with Rotatinski- Aschoff bodies, and findings of acute or chronic cholecystitis. True adenomatous polyps have a glandular histology. Malignant polyps can demonstrate cancerous changes characteristic of adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and adenoacanthoma. The degree of malignant differentiation usually correlates to polyp size. [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Juvenile Xanthogranuloma  -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Juvenile xanthogranulomas are most commonly found in early childhood with >15-20% of patients having lesions at birth. It is estimated that in 75% of cases, lesions appear during the first year of life. [1] Approximately 10% of cases may appear in adulthood and are known as adult xanthogranulo ma . [1] In children, the disease most often affects males; however, in adults, there is no sex prevalence. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Gallbladder Carcinoma -- Etiology -- Gallbladder Carcinoma Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Areas with endemic Salmonella Typhi and Helicobacter pylori infection [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Grey Turner Sign -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Although historically associated with severe acute necrotizing pancreatitis, Grey Turner sign is nonspecific. It may be present with almost any condition causing intra-abdominal or retroperitoneal bleeding, and the differential diagnosis is quite extensive. Some commonly found etiologies include acute appendicitis, ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm, ruptured ectopic pregnancy, splenic rupture following trauma or infectious mononucleosis, various malignancies such as bleeding intra-abdominal metastases, metastatic thyroid and esophageal cancer, metastatic liver cancer, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, ruptured hepatocellular carcinoma, amoebic liver abscess, perforated gastric or duodenal ulcer, peri-renal hemorrhage, common bile duct rupture, biliary peritonitis, perirenal hemorrhage, acute pyelonephritis, and hemorrhagic ascites among others. In cases of acute peri-renal hemorrhage, the etiology is most commonly a renal malignancy in around 70% of cases, with other causes being related to preeclampsia, polyarteritis nodosa, infection, nephrosclerosis, a ruptured cyst, and spontaneous hemorrhage. [3] [12] [13] [15] [16] [17] [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2645
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, benign are regarded as not a finding of disease, indicating that the presence of benign tumor cells does not constitute a pathological diagnosis. In contrast, the category cancer; other and unspecified primary is classified as a type of malignant tumor (ورم صماوي خبيث), reflecting its nature as a cancerous condition. Recognizing that Tumor cells, benign are not disease findings while cancer; other and unspecified primary represents malignant neoplasia helps clinicians differentiate non‑pathogenic cellular changes from clinically significant cancers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1213
retrieved_docs.content: Xanthoma due to primary hypercholesterolemia is classified under disorders of lipid metabolism, indicating that this cutaneous lesion represents a manifestation of abnormal lipid processing associated with primary hypercholesterolemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2508
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, benign are regarded as not a finding in cancer of ovary, cancer; other and unspecified primary, and in السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer). Conversely, tumor cells, malignant and tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant are considered abnormal cells of disease in the same three malignancies—cancer of ovary, cancer; other and unspecified primary, and السرطان البنكرياسي. This distinction highlights that benign‑appearing tumor cells do not represent disease involvement in these cancers, whereas malignant or indeterminate tumor cells are identified as the pathological cellular component of each condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2581
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, malignant and tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant are both classified as abnormal cells of disease and are linked to cancer; other and unspecified primary, with a specific association to pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2898
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, benign are not considered a finding of the disease category cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that benign tumor cells do not represent this type of cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2510
retrieved_docs.content: Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant, is linked to the Arabic term “النقيلة,” indicating that this word is used to refer to a lesion whose benign or malignant nature has not yet been determined.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2918
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells represent the abnormal cellular component that defines and drives the disease known as السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that the presence of these atypical, cancerous cells is the hallmark of this specific malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1108
retrieved_docs.content: Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant, is linked to النقيلة, and النقيلة is in turn associated with سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس. This indicates that an indeterminate neoplasm may involve metastatic processes, and such metastasis can manifest as metastatic cancer in the head of the pancreas, highlighting the importance of evaluating metastatic potential when the benign‑malignant nature of a neoplasm is unclear.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1923
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي is a medical condition that is associated with ذَبْهَةُ برنزميتال. Clinically, التصلب العصيدي is linked to several findings, including النفخة الانقباضية, حفظ الصحة الروتيني, عرج, and لغط الشريان السباتي. In addition, التصلب العصيدي has a recognized subtype called التصلب العصيدي المعمم, reflecting a more generalized form of the disease. These associations are important for recognizing the clinical presentation of التصلب العصيدي and guiding appropriate evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, represent the abnormal cellular component of السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer). Their ambiguous nature underscores the diagnostic challenge in distinguishing benign from malignant lesions within the pancreas, influencing treatment decisions and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2497
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a diagnostic finding for several malignant conditions, including cancer of ovary, cancer; other and unspecified primary, and السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer). In contrast, Tumor cells, benign are explicitly noted as not being a finding for these same diseases, indicating that the presence of benign tumor cells does not support a diagnosis of ovarian cancer, other unspecified primary cancers, or pancreatic cancer. Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant are classified as abnormal cells of disease for cancer of ovary, cancer; other and unspecified primary, and السرطان البنكرياسي, reflecting that cells of indeterminate malignant potential are considered pathologic indicators associated with these cancers. Together, these relationships clarify that malignant cellular infiltrates serve as positive evidence of cancer, benign tumor cells are negative evidence, and cells of uncertain behavior represent abnormal cellular findings linked to ovarian, unspecified primary, and pancreatic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1905
retrieved_docs.content: Benign tumor cells are not regarded as a finding of pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي), indicating that their presence does not support a diagnosis of this malignancy. In contrast, tumor cells whose nature is uncertain—whether benign or malignant—are considered abnormal cells of pancreatic cancer, reflecting their potential relevance to the disease process. Recognizing the difference between clearly benign cells and cells of indeterminate behavior is essential for accurate pathological assessment and appropriate patient management in the context of pancreatic cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell carcinoma), which is broadly related to مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ (jaundiced liver disease). This connection indicates that hepatocellular carcinoma often presents within the spectrum of liver diseases that cause jaundice, highlighting the importance of recognizing jaundice as a potential clinical clue to underlying malignant liver pathology. Understanding this relationship aids clinicians in early detection, accurate diagnosis, and appropriate management of patients with liver‑related malignancies and associated jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a finding of disease in both cancer of ovary and in the pancreatic cancer referred to in Arabic as السرطان البنكرياسي. Likewise, Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are described as abnormal cells of disease for both cancer of ovary and السرطان البنكرياسي. These connections illustrate that certain histopathologic features—such as malignant cellular infiltrates and tumor cells of indeterminate benign‑malignant status—can appear across different malignancies, highlighting the importance of careful pathological assessment to accurately diagnose and manage ovarian and pancreatic cancers.
